clean_story:
Pain’s volume knob. Your brain adjusts it.

The phrase "expectation shapes pain" comes from how the brain predicts harm before it happens—like a thermostat set too high or low, changing how much heat you feel.

A soldier in battle might not notice a bullet wound until the fight ends. Same injury, same nerves, but adrenaline and focus dial the pain down. Flip it: Wait for a dentist’s drill while staring at the needle, and your brain cranks the pain up before it starts. fMRI scans show this: The anterior cingulate cortex (pain’s alarm system) lights up brighter when people expect agony, even if the actual stimulus stays identical.

Placebos work here too. Give someone a sugar pill they believe is morphine, and their brain releases real endorphins—natural painkillers. Nocebos do the opposite: Tell a patient a harmless cream might burn, and their skin reddens as if scalded. Studies with electric shocks prove it: Subjects who saw a "high pain" warning rated identical zaps as twice as painful.

Pain isn’t just damage talking. It’s your brain’s guesswork—mixing memory, fear, and context into the final sensation.

Three related subjects are placebo mechanics, fear-pain feedback loops, and how language alters suffering.
